checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the output JSON against the source PDF. All paragraphs and sentences in the output have a clear basis in the source material. AI-generated titles, alt text, and table summaries are within the allowed 'Acceptable Changes'. There are no fabricated sections or invented text blocks.

All URLs found in the output (e.g., nctech.org, youtube.com, statescoop.com, nascio.org, NC.gov) are present in the source PDF text or correspond to hyperlinks embedded in the text. There are no fabricated phone numbers, email addresses, or physical addresses.

All 28 pages of the source PDF are accounted for in the output. All major sections, including front and back covers, the Secretary's letter, financial information, goal highlights, and awards, are present and represented in the correct sequence.

The output uses table structures to manage multi-column layouts found on pages 16, 17, 18, and 26. These are layout-driven tables rather than data grids. All content points (bullets) from the source are included within these structures. No data rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text content within each section is complete. I cross-referenced the bulleted lists in the 'Leveraging IT Resources for Better Decision Making' and 'New Applications' sections, and all items from the source are present in the output. There are no significant omissions of paragraphs or key sentences.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Wording changes are minimal and preserve the original meaning. Typos from the source (e.g., 'Recieve' on page 24) are faithfully preserved. Numbers and dates (e.g., fiscal years, dollar amounts in the financial section) match the source perfectly.

checks.a_analysis: The output was scanned for any unintended text repetitions. The text '2020 / 2021' appears on both the cover (Page 1) and the final page (Page 28), which accurately reflects the source document. No instances of accidental content duplication were identified.

Several major section headings and subheadings from the source document (Pages 17 and 18) have been incorrectly converted into plain text within table cells rather than being identified as heading elements. For example, 'Modernize and centralize IT operations' (a major H2 heading on Page 18) and subheadings like 'New Applications', 'Constituent Portal', and 'First Responder Emerging Technologies Program' are all embedded as text inside tables 25 and 26. This effectively removes them from the document's navigable hierarchy.
checks.b_evidence_source: Modernize and centralize IT operations (Page 18, major heading); New Applications (Page 17, subheading).
checks.b_evidence_output: The text 'Modernize and centralize IT operations' is used as a table caption for element index 26, and subheadings like 'New Applications' are plain text in table cells (Element index 25, Row 1, Cell 1).
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections 'Cultivate our IT workforce' and 'Modernize and centralize IT operations', element indices 25 and 26.

checks.a_analysis: Tables 23, 25, and 26 are structurally corrupted. Instead of representing structured data, these tables were used to manage multi-column layouts from the source, resulting in single sentences and list items being split across different rows and cells. For example, in Table 23, a sentence is broken between Row 1 Cell 1 and Row 1 Cell 2. This is a fundamental failure of table structure identification.
checks.b_evidence_source: Built an inspection reporting tool for inspectors to record all aspects of an asphalt surface treatment on iPads, even when working in remote environments without a cellular signal. (Page 16)
checks.b_evidence_output:
Row 1, Cell 1: '...even when working in remote'
Row 1, Cell 2: 'environments without a cellular signal...'
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 'Leveraging IT Resources for Better Decision Making', element index 23.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order is severely disrupted by the corrupted table structures in elements 23, 25, and 26. Because sentences are split across table cells, a standard top-to-bottom or left-to-right reading of the table results in fragmented and logically broken information flow. This makes the content incomprehensible for users, particularly those using screen readers who rely on logical linear flow.
checks.b_evidence_source: Built a mobile-first, public-facing request system for the N.C. Division of Motor Vehicles’ License and Theft Bureau, transforming its operations with more than 10,000 submissions in the first nine months of operations (Page 17)
checks.b_evidence_output:
Row 1, Cell 1: '...more than 10,000 submissions in'
Row 1, Cell 2: 'the first nine months of operations'
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 'Cultivate our IT workforce', element index 25.

checks.a_analysis: While many lists from the source were incorrectly converted into table content (as noted in S3), the list elements that were correctly identified (such as the 'Goals' list in element index 12 and sidebar statistics in index 30 and 36) maintain correct structure and meaning.

The output correctly captures and points to all valid URLs found in the source document, including state government sites (NC.gov), social media links (YouTube), and organizational sites (NCTech, StateScoop, NASCIO). No broken or fabricated links were discovered.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly maintains the visual organization and flow of information from the source PDF. The transition from the multi-column and graphically dense layout of the PDF to a single-column responsive HTML layout is executed well. Sections follow the original order, and the hierarchy of information is preserved. Users can find the same information in the same relative sequence.

The source document does not contain traditional grid-based tables. Data and lists are presented as formatted text and bullet points, which have been correctly converted into HTML lists and paragraphs.

Several meaningful images are missing or broken in the HTML output. Most significantly, the pie charts providing data visualization for 'Budget Expenditures' (PDF page 11) and 'Budget Funding Sources' (PDF page 12) are completely missing. Additionally, all photographic content (e.g., photos of Secretary Weaver on page 3, Governor Cooper on page 21, and staff throughout) are rendered as solid green placeholder blocks instead of the actual images.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains meaningful images including a 'Budget Expenditures' pie chart on page 11, a 'Budget Funding Sources' pie chart on page 12, and various photos such as Secretary Jim Weaver on page 3 and Governor Roy Cooper on page 21.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML output, the pie charts from pages 11 and 12 are missing entirely. The photos from the report are rendered as solid green placeholder blocks.
checks.b_evidence_location: Financial Information section (pages 11-12), From the Secretary section (page 3), and throughout the document.

checks.a_analysis: There is no visual duplication of body content in the HTML output. The appearance of headings in the Table of Contents and then again as section headers in the body is standard accessible design and does not constitute a defect.
